Just now

--

Three questions that defined SuperAI Singapore 2026
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Earlier this year, NVIDIA made an observation that felt impossible to ignore at SuperAI Singapore:
“For my team, the cost of compute is far beyond the costs of the employees.”
For the past two years, much of the AI conversation focused on experimentation and capability.
Remember when AI felt almost free?
Today, the question is no longer what AI can do, but whether the outcomes justify the costs.
Many conversations at SuperAI Singapore 2026 seemed to converge on that tension.
Sitting down with WEKA, TuringData, Simular and Nansen, and listening to keynotes from industry pioneers, three questions kept resurfacing.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Can we afford it?
Across SuperAI, conversations increasingly revolved around AI memory and data movement, underscoring Benedict Evans’ point that deploying AI at scale is becoming as important as building it.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Benedict EvansCan we trust it?
Trust becomes the scarce resource in an AI-rich world, Balaji Srinivasan argued; and as superintelligence gathers pace, Max Tegmark challenged us to build “pro-human” AI.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Balaji SrinivasanPress enter or click to view image in full size

Max TegmarkWhat becomes possible?
AI and crypto are beginning to intersect around identity & ownership as AI agents transact on our behalf; meanwhile, we may be entering a transition from software designed for humans to software designed for AI.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Press enter or click to view image in full size

One thought-provoking demo
Not a bigger model or a faster chip, but a brain-computer interface art project driven by brain signals that augmented — and possibly expanded the boundaries of — human creativity.
For all the awe inspired by the technology, the human remains at the centre of AI’s story — a conclusion echoed by many of the guests I spoke with.
